title: P-value in a two-sample z test for the difference of proportions
description: Calculate the P-value in a two-sample z test for the difference of proportions.
topic_path: Testing for the difference of two population proportions
P-value in a two-sample z test for the difference of proportions

assessment_items.question_content:
A large school district was considering changing the start times to its schools. The change would involve high schools starting later and elementary schools starting earlier. The school district was curious if parents supported this change, so they surveyed separate random samples of parents from both types of school in the district. Here are their results:

Supports change? | High school | Elementary
- | -: | -:
Yes | $54$ | $24$
No | $46$ | $26$
**Total** | $100$ | $50$

The school district wants to use these results to test if the proportion of parents who support the change is different between these types of schools. They will test $H_0: p_\text{HS} = p_\text{E}$ versus $H_a: p_\text{HS} \neq p_\text{E}$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

We'll use the sample results to first calculate the **test statistic** which we'll then use to calculate the **P-value**.

###Calculating the test statistic

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 3]]

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

Type of school | High school | Elementary
- | -: | -:
Supports change | $\blueD{54}$ | $\greenD{24}$
Doesn't support | $46$ | $26$
**Total** | $\purpleD{100}$ | $\goldD{50}$

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{54}+\greenD{24}}{\purpleD{100}+\goldD{50}}=0.52 \\\\
\hat p_\text{HS}&=\dfrac{\blueD{54}}{\purpleD{100}}=0.54 \\\\
\hat p_\text{E}&=\dfrac{\greenD{24}}{\goldD{50}}=0.48
\end{align}$

They want to test $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{HS}} \neq p_{\text{E}}$. We'll use $\hat p_{\text{HS}}-\hat p_{\text{E}}$ for the sample difference in the test statistic:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{(0.54-0.48)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.52(0.48)}{100}+\dfrac{0.52(0.48)}{50}}} \\\\
&\approx\dfrac{0.06}{0.087} \\\\
&\approx 0.693
\end{align}$

###Calculating the P-value

Their alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{HS}} \neq p_{\text{E}}$. This is a two-sided test, so the P-value is the area *above* their test statistic $z\approx 0.693$ combined with the area *below* $z \approx -0.693$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. H sub a is p sub 1 does not equal p sub 2. Test statistics are marked before the left and right tails at z approximately equals negative 0.69 and z approximately equals 0.69.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/db2bf0775de78f34c02248e4ac519cc6de5d430a)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z>0.693$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0.693 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\\\
&\text{output} \approx 0.244
\end{align}$

We can multiply that output by $2$ to get the combined area in both tails:

$\begin{align}
\text{P-value} &\approx 0.244 \times 2 \text{ tails} \\\\
&\approx 0.488
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Answer

The P-value is greater than $0.15$.

$P\text{-value} \geq 0.15$

assessment_items.question_content:
Market researchers wondered if teens were more or less likely than adults to use their mobile phone as an alarm clock. The researchers surveyed a random sample of people and removed those who didn't have a mobile phone. Here are the results:

Use phone as alarm? | Teen | Adult
- | -: | -:
Yes | $35$ | $90$
No | $15$ | $60$
**Total** | $50$ | $150$

The researchers want to test if these results suggest a significant difference between the proportions of teens and adults that use their phone as an alarm clock. They will test $H_0: p_\text{T} = p_\text{A}$ versus $H_a: p_\text{T} \neq p_\text{A}$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

We'll use the sample results to first calculate the **test statistic** which we'll then use to calculate the **P-value**.

###Calculating the test statistic

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

Use phone as alarm? | Teen | Adult
- | -: | -:
Yes | $\blueD{35}$ | $\greenD{90}$
No | $15$ | $60$
**Total** | $\purpleD{50}$ | $\goldD{150}$

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{35}+\greenD{90}}{\purpleD{50}+\goldD{150}}=0.625 \\\\
\hat p_\text{T}&=\dfrac{\blueD{35}}{\purpleD{50}}=0.7 \\\\
\hat p_\text{A}&=\dfrac{\greenD{90}}{\goldD{150}}=0.6
\end{align}$

They want to test $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{T}} \neq p_{\text{A}}$. We'll use $\hat p_{\text{T}}-\hat p_{\text{A}}$ for the sample difference in the test statistic:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{(0.7-0.6)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.625(0.375)}{50}+\dfrac{0.625(0.375)}{150}}} \\\\
&\approx\dfrac{0.1}{0.079} \\\\
&\approx 1.265
\end{align}$

###Calculating the P-value

Their alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{T}} \neq p_{\text{A}}$. This is a two-sided test, so the P-value is the area *above* their test statistic $z\approx 1.265$ combined with the area *below* $z \approx -1.265$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. H sub a is p sub 1 does not equal p sub 2. Test statistics are marked under the left and right tails at z approximately equals negative 1.265 and z approximately equals 1.265.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d83c3fe9534cb7d79ed3303d091705e7172e433)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z>1.265$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 1.265\\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\\\
&\text{output} \approx 0.103
\end{align}$

We can multiply that output by $2$ to get the combined area in both tails:

$\begin{align}
\text{P-value} &\approx 0.103 \times 2 \text{ tails} \\\\
&\approx 0.206
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Answer

The P-value is greater than $0.15$.

$P\text{-value} \geq 0.15$

assessment_items.question_content:
Joshua plays basketball, and he learned a new technique for shooting free throws. He decided to shoot $50$ free throws with his old technique and $50$ with the new technique to see if there was a difference in his performance. He made $30$ of $50$ free throws using his old technique, and he made $38$ of $50$ using the new technique. He took these shots over multiple days and alternated techniques, so he's willing to assume his data satisfy the conditions for inference.

Joshua wants to test if these results suggest a significant difference between the proportions of free throws made with each technique. He will test $H_0: p_\text{New} = p_\text{Old}$ versus $H_a: p_\text{New} \neq p_\text{Old}$.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

We'll use the sample results to first calculate the **test statistic** which we'll then use to calculate the **P-value**.

###Calculating the test statistic

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

He made $\greenD{30}$ of $\goldD{50}$ free throws using his old technique, and he made $\blueD{38}$ of $\purpleD{50}$ using the new technique.

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{38}+\greenD{30}}{\purpleD{50}+\goldD{50}}=0.68 \\\\
\hat p_\text{New}&=\dfrac{\blueD{38}}{\purpleD{50}}=0.76 \\\\
\hat p_\text{Old}&=\dfrac{\greenD{30}}{\goldD{50}}=0.60
\end{align}$

He wants to test $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{New}} \neq p_{\text{Old}}$. We'll use $\hat p_{\text{New}}-\hat p_{\text{Old}}$ for the sample difference in the test statistic:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{(0.76-0.60)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.68(0.32)}{50}+\dfrac{0.68(0.32)}{50}}} \\\\
&\approx\dfrac{0.16}{0.093} \\\\
&\approx 1.715
\end{align}$

###Calculating the P-value

His alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{New}} \neq p_{\text{Old}}$. This is a two-sided test, so the P-value is the area *above* his test statistic $z\approx 1.715$ combined with the area *below* $z \approx -1.715$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. H sub a is p sub 1 does not equal p sub 2. Test statistics are marked under the left and right tails at z approximately equals negative 1.715 and z approximately equals 1.715.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0c43293b34e7c60908d52010a5b14fe57a443882)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z>1.715$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 1.715\\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\\\
&\text{output} \approx 0.043
\end{align}$

We can multiply that output by $2$ to get the combined area in both tails:

$\begin{align}
\text{P-value} &\approx 0.043 \times 2 \text{ tails} \\\\
&\approx 0.086
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Answer

The P-value is between $0.05$ and $0.10$.

$0.05 \leq P\text{-value}<0.10$

assessment_items.question_content:
A medical researcher was curious if the proportion of adult males who are color-blind is the same in the United States and Great Britain. They obtained separate random samples of records for adult men from each country. Here are the sample results:

Country | United States | Great Britain
- | -: | -:
Color-blind | $30$ | $10$
Not | $270$ | $190$
**Total** | $300$ | $200$

The researcher wants to use these results to test if the proportion of adult men who are color-blind is significantly different in the two countries. They will test $H_0: p_\text{US} = p_\text{GB}$ versus $H_a: p_\text{US} \neq p_\text{GB}$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

We'll use the sample results to first calculate the **test statistic** which we'll then use to calculate the **P-value**.

###Calculating the test statistic

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

Country | United States| Great Britain
- | -: | -:
Color-blind | $\blueD{30}$ | $\greenD{10}$
Not | $270$ | $190$
**Total** | $\purpleD{300}$ | $\goldD{200}$

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{30}+\greenD{10}}{\purpleD{300}+\goldD{200}}=0.08 \\\\
\hat p_\text{US}&=\dfrac{\blueD{30}}{\purpleD{300}}=0.10 \\\\
\hat p_\text{GB}&=\dfrac{\greenD{10}}{\goldD{200}}=0.05
\end{align}$

The researcher wants to test $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{US}} \neq p_{\text{GB}}$. We'll use $\hat p_{\text{US}}-\hat p_{\text{GB}}$ for the sample difference in the test statistic:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{(0.10-0.05)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.08(0.92)}{300}+\dfrac{0.08(0.92)}{200}}} \\\\
&\approx\dfrac{0.05}{0.024766} \\\\
&\approx 2.02
\end{align}$

###Calculating the P-value

The researcher's alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{US}} \neq p_{\text{GB}}$. This is a two-sided test, so the P-value is the area *above* their test statistic $z\approx 2.02$ combined with the area *below* $z \approx -2.02$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. H sub a is p sub 1 minus p sub 2 does not equal 0. Test statistics are marked under the left and right tails at z approximately equals negative 2.02 and z approximately equals 2.02.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1a865d8d151cb182afe46c0eaf93ee24c90d6c7a)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z>2.02$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 2.02\\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\\\
&\text{output} \approx 0.0217
\end{align}$

We can multiply that output by $2$ to get the combined area in both tails:

$\begin{align}
\text{P-value} &\approx 0.0217 \times 2 \text{ tails} \\\\
&\approx 0.0434
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Answer

The P-value is between $0.01$ and $0.05$.

$0.01 \leq P\text{-value}<0.05$

assessment_items.question_content:
A political consultant wondered if support for a candidate was significantly different between men and women. The consultant surveyed a random sample of voters. Here are the results:

Supports candidate? | Men | Women
- | -: | -:
Yes | $24$ | $48$
No | $56$ | $52$
**Total** | $80$ | $100$

The consultant wants to test if these results suggest a significant difference in support between men and women. They will test $H_0: p_\text{W} = p_\text{M}$ versus $H_a: p_\text{W} \neq p_\text{M}$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

We'll use the sample results to first calculate the **test statistic** which we'll then use to calculate the **P-value**.

###Calculating the test statistic

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

Supports candidate? | Men | Women
- | -: | -:
Yes | $\greenD{24}$ | $\blueD{48}$
No | $56$ | $52$
**Total** | $\goldD{80}$ | $\purpleD{100}$

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{48}+\greenD{24}}{\purpleD{100}+\goldD{80}}=0.4 \\\\
\hat p_\text{W}&=\dfrac{\blueD{48}}{\purpleD{100}}=0.48 \\\\
\hat p_\text{M}&=\dfrac{\greenD{24}}{\goldD{80}}=0.3
\end{align}$

The consultant wants to test $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{W}} \neq p_{\text{M}}$. We'll use $\hat p_{\text{W}}-\hat p_{\text{M}}$ for the sample difference in the test statistic:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{(0.48-0.3)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.4(0.6)}{100}+\dfrac{0.4(0.6)}{80}}} \\\\
&\approx\dfrac{0.18}{0.073} \\\\
&\approx 2.45
\end{align}$

###Calculating the P-value

The consultant's alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{W}} \neq p_{\text{M}}$. This is a two-sided test, so the P-value is the area *above* their test statistic $z\approx 2.45$ combined with the area *below* $z \approx -2.45$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. H sub a is p sub 1 does not equal p sub 2. Test statistics are marked under the left and right tails at z approximately equals negative 2.45 and z approximately equals 2.45.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b7fea29e6f90a2a4a943ad6cabf0999a266100b0)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z>2.45$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 2.45\\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\\\
&\text{output} \approx 0.007
\end{align}$

We can multiply that output by $2$ to get the combined area in both tails:

$\begin{align}
\text{P-value} &\approx 0.007 \times 2 \text{ tails} \\\\
&\approx 0.014
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Answer

The P-value is between $0.01$ and $0.05$.

$0.01 \leq P\text{-value}<0.05$

assessment_items.question_content:
A political consultant wondered if support for a candidate was significantly different between voters who owned a landline telephone and voters who did not. The consultant surveyed a random sample of voters and found $36$ of $75$ who owned a landline supported the candidate, while $27$ of $100$ who did not own a landline supported the candidate.

The consultant wants to test if these results suggest a significant difference in support between voters who do and do not own a landline. They will test $H_0: p_\text{Landline} = p_\text{No landline}$ versus $H_a: p_\text{Landline} \neq p_\text{No landline}$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

We'll use the sample results to first calculate the **test statistic** which we'll then use to calculate the **P-value**.

###Calculating the test statistic

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

The consultant surveyed a random sample of voters and found $\blueD{36}$ of $\purpleD{75}$ who owned a landline supported the candidate, while $\greenD{27}$ of $\goldD{100}$ who did not own a landline supported the candidate.

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{36}+\greenD{27}}{\purpleD{75}+\goldD{100}}=0.36 \\\\
\hat p_\text{Landline}&=\dfrac{\blueD{36}}{\purpleD{75}}=0.48 \\\\
\hat p_\text{No landline}&=\dfrac{\greenD{27}}{\goldD{100}}=0.27
\end{align}$

The consultant wants to test $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{Landline}} \neq p_{\text{No landline}}$. We'll use $\hat p_{\text{Landline}}-\hat p_{\text{No landline}}$ for the sample difference in the test statistic:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{(0.48-0.27)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.36(0.64)}{75}+\dfrac{0.36(0.64)}{100}}} \\\\
&\approx\dfrac{0.21}{0.073} \\\\
&\approx 2.86
\end{align}$

###Calculating the P-value

The consultant's alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{Landline}} \neq p_{\text{No landline}}$. This is a two-sided test, so the P-value is the area *above* their test statistic $z\approx 2.86$ combined with the area *below* $z \approx -2.86$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. H sub a is p sub 1 does not equal p sub 2. Test statistics are marked under the left and right tails at z approximately equals negative 2.86 and z approximately equals 2.86.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/067436d9a32e18cd15d1889c2957281245ca1139)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z>2.86$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 2.86\\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\\\
&\text{output} \approx 0.002
\end{align}$

We can multiply that output by $2$ to get the combined area in both tails:

$\begin{align}
\text{P-value} &\approx 0.002 \times 2 \text{ tails} \\\\
&\approx 0.004
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Answer

The P-value is less than $0.01$.

$P\text{-value}<0.01$

assessment_items.question_content:
Researchers suspect that myopia, or nearsightedness, is becoming more common over time. A study from the year $2000$ showed $132$ cases of myopia in $400$ randomly selected people. A separate study from $2015$ showed $228$ cases in $600$ randomly selected people.

The researchers want to use these results to test $H_0\text{: }p_{2015}-p_{2000}=0$ versus $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_{2015}-p_{2000}>0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

We'll use the sample results to first calculate the **test statistic** which we'll then use to calculate the **P-value**.

###Calculating the test statistic

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

A study from the year $2000$ showed $\greenD{132}$ cases of myopia in $\goldD{400}$ randomly selected people. A separate study from $2015$ showed $\blueD{228}$ cases in $\purpleD{600}$ randomly selected people.

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{228}+\greenD{132}}{\purpleD{600}+\goldD{400}}=0.36 \\\\
\hat p_{2015}&=\dfrac{\blueD{228}}{\purpleD{600}}=0.38 \\\\
\hat p_{2000}&=\dfrac{\greenD{132}}{\goldD{400}}=0.33
\end{align}$

The researchers want to test $H_\text{a}:p_{2015} -
 p_{2000}>0$, so we'll use $\hat p_{2015}-\hat p_{2000}$ for the sample difference in the test statistic:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{(0.38-0.33)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.36(0.64)}{600}+\dfrac{0.36(0.64)}{400}}} \\\\
&\approx\dfrac{0.05}{0.031} \\\\
&\approx 1.614
\end{align}$

###Calculating the P-value

Their alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}:p_{2015} -
 p_{2000}>0$. This is a one-sided test, so the P-value is the area *above* their test statistic $z \approx 1.614$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. H sub a is p sub 1 minus p sub 2 greater than 0. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at z approximately equals 1.614.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/518fc4eaa81b479b3135e527fdb34e83fa3b2850)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z>1.614$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 1.614 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.053
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The P-value is between $0.05$ and $0.10$.

$0.05 \leq P\text{-value}<0.10$

assessment_items.question_content:
Nicki's business ships orders from a location in Chicago and another location in Denver. She suspected that a larger proportion of orders shipped from the Denver location were arriving late. She took a random sample of $100$ orders from each location. She found that $20$ orders in the Denver sample arrived late, and $12$ orders in the Chicago sample arrived late.

She wants to use these results to test if the proportion of orders that arrive late is significantly larger at the Denver location. She will test $H_0: p_\text{Denver} = p_\text{Chicago}$ versus $H_a: p_\text{Denver} > p_\text{Chicago}$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

We'll use the sample results to first calculate the **test statistic** which we'll then use to calculate the **P-value**.

###Calculating the test statistic

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

She took a random sample of $100$ orders from each location. She found that $\blueD{20}$ orders in the Denver sample arrived late, and $\greenD{12}$ orders in the Chicago sample arrived late.

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{20}+\greenD{12}}{100+100}=0.16 \\\\
\hat p_\text{Denver}&=\dfrac{\blueD{20}}{100}=0.20 \\\\
\hat p_\text{Chicago}&=\dfrac{\greenD{12}}{100}=0.12
\end{align}$

She wants to test $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{Denver}}>p_{\text{Chicago}}$, so we'll use $\hat p_{\text{Denver}}-\hat p_{\text{Chicago}}$ for the sample difference in the test statistic:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{(0.20-0.12)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.16(1-0.16)}{100}+\dfrac{0.16(1-0.16)}{100}}} \\\\
&\approx\dfrac{0.08}{0.052} \\\\
&\approx 1.543
\end{align}$

###Calculating the P-value

Nicki's alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{Denver}}>p_{\text{Chicago}}$, which is equivalent to $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{Denver}}-p_{\text{Chicago}}>0$. This is a one-sided test, so the P-value is the area *above* her test statistic $z \approx 1.543$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. H sub a is p sub 1 minus p sub 2 greater than 0. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at z approximately equals 1.543.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1c0902c64e2424892e2454c16bda10ef7c7c8a1d)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z>1.543$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 1.543 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.061
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The P-value is between $0.05$ and $0.10$.

$0.05 \leq P\text{-value}<0.10$

assessment_items.question_content:
An economist was comparing unemployment rates between states. A random sample of $800$ adults in Wisconsin showed that $20$ were unemployed, and a random sample of  $800$ adults in Minnesota showed that $28$ were unemployed.

The economist want to use these results to test $H_0\text{: }p_\text{MN}-p_\text{WI}=0$ versus $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_\text{MN}-p_\text{WI}>0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

We'll use the sample results to first calculate the **test statistic** which we'll then use to calculate the **P-value**.

###Calculating the test statistic

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

A random sample of $\goldD{800}$ adults in Wisconsin showed that $\greenD{20}$ were unemployed, and a random sample of  $\purpleD{800}$ adults in Minnesota showed that $\blueD{28}$ were unemployed.

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{28}+\greenD{20}}{\purpleD{800}+\goldD{800}}=0.03 \\\\
\hat p_\text{MN}&=\dfrac{\blueD{28}}{\purpleD{800}}=0.035 \\\\
\hat p_\text{WI}&=\dfrac{\greenD{20}}{\goldD{800}}=0.025
\end{align}$

They want to test $H_\text{a}:p_\text{MN} -
 p_\text{WI}>0$, so we'll use $\hat p_\text{MN}-\hat p_\text{WI}$ for the sample difference in the test statistic:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{(0.035-0.025)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.03(0.97)}{800}+\dfrac{0.03(0.97)}{800}}} \\\\
&\approx\dfrac{0.01}{0.009} \\\\
&\approx 1.172
\end{align}$

###Calculating the P-value

Their alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}:p_\text{MN} -
 p_\text{WI}>0$. This is a one-sided test, so the P-value is the area *above* their test statistic $z \approx 1.172$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. H sub a is p sub 1 minus p sub 2 greater than 0. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at z approximately equals 1.172.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1cecd61ccd3cf0d77f066f983a0bfd76ae7f5d84)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z>1.172$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 1.172 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.121
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The P-value is between $0.10$ and $0.15$.

$0.10 \leq P\text{-value}<0.15$

assessment_items.question_content:
Researchers studying osteoporosis (bone loss) suspected that women over the age of $50$ in the United States are diagnosed with the disease more often than women over $50$ in Mexico. They took a random sample of $200$ women over the age of $50$ from each country. Here are the results:

Diagnosed with  osteoporosis? | US | Mexico
- | -: | -:
Yes | $40$ | $20$
No | $160$ | $180$
**Total** | $200$ | $200$

The researchers want to use these results to test $H_0\text{: }p_\text{US}-p_\text{M}=0$ versus $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_\text{US}-p_\text{M}>0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

We'll use the sample results to first calculate the **test statistic** which we'll then use to calculate the **P-value**.

###Calculating the test statistic

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

Has osteoporosis? | US | Mexico
- | -: | -:
Yes | $\blueD{40}$ | $\greenD{20}$
No | $160$ | $180$
**Total** | $\purpleD{200}$ | $\goldD{200}$

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{40}+\greenD{20}}{\purpleD{200}+\goldD{200}}=0.15 \\\\
\hat p_\text{US}&=\dfrac{\blueD{40}}{\purpleD{200}}=0.2 \\\\
\hat p_\text{M}&=\dfrac{\greenD{20}}{\goldD{200}}=0.1
\end{align}$

They want to test $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{US}} - p_{\text{M}}>0$, so we'll use $\hat p_{\text{US}}-\hat p_{\text{M}}$ for the sample difference in the test statistic:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{(0.2-0.1)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.15(0.85)}{200}+\dfrac{0.15(0.85)}{200}}} \\\\
&\approx\dfrac{0.1}{0.036} \\\\
&\approx 2.801
\end{align}$

###Calculating the P-value

Their alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{US}}-p_{\text{M}}>0$. This is a one-sided test, so the P-value is the area *above* their test statistic $z \approx 2.801$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. H sub a is p sub 1 minus p sub 2 greater than 0. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at z approximately equals 2.801.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/44a3ccc6dbd4d80aa2422cafaf896b6e150fb971)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z>2.801$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 2.801 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.003
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The P-value is less than $0.01$.

$P\text{-value}<0.01$

assessment_items.question_content:
An economist was curious if women were more satisfied with their jobs than men. A random sample of $220$ workers showed that $46$ of $100$ women were satisfied with their jobs, and $42$ of $120$ men were satisfied.

The economist want to use these results to test $H_0\text{: }p_\text{W}-p_\text{M}=0$ versus $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_\text{W}-p_\text{M}>0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

We'll use the sample results to first calculate the **test statistic** which we'll then use to calculate the **P-value**.

###Calculating the test statistic

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

A random sample of $220$ workers showed that $\blueD{46}$ of $\purpleD{100}$ women were satisfied with their jobs, and $\greenD{42}$ of $\goldD{120}$ men were satisfied.

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{46}+\greenD{42}}{\purpleD{100}+\goldD{120}}=0.4 \\\\
\hat p_\text{W}&=\dfrac{\blueD{46}}{\purpleD{100}}=0.46 \\\\
\hat p_\text{M}&=\dfrac{\greenD{42}}{\goldD{120}}=0.35
\end{align}$

They want to test $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{W}} - p_{\text{M}}>0$, so we'll use $\hat p_{\text{W}}-\hat p_{\text{M}}$ for the sample difference in the test statistic:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{(0.46-0.35)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.4(0.6)}{100}+\dfrac{0.4(0.6)}{120}}} \\\\
&\approx\dfrac{0.11}{0.066} \\\\
&\approx 1.658
\end{align}$

###Calculating the P-value

Their alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{W}}-p_{\text{M}}>0$. This is a one-sided test, so the P-value is the area *above* their test statistic $z \approx 1.658$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. H sub a is p sub 1 minus p sub 2 greater than 0. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at z approximately equals 1.658.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/992561c996abde7f41a729dd4de08f766f964222)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z>1.658$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 1.658 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.049
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The P-value is between $0.01$ and $0.05$.

$0.01 \leq P\text{-value}<0.05$

assessment_items.question_content:
Caroline thinks she can flip a coin so it lands showing heads more often with her right hand. She flipped a coin $50$ times with each hand. Here the results:

Result | Right | Left
- | -: | -:
Heads | $30$ | $20$
Tails | $20$ | $30$
**Total** | $50$ | $50$

Caroline wants to use these results to test $H_0\text{: }p_\text{R}-p_\text{L}=0$ versus $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_\text{R}-p_\text{L}>0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

We'll use the sample results to first calculate the **test statistic** which we'll then use to calculate the **P-value**.

###Calculating the test statistic

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

Result | Right | Left
- | -: | -:
Heads | $\blueD{30}$ | $\greenD{20}$
Tails | $20$ | $30$
**Total** | $\purpleD{50}$ | $\goldD{50}$

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{30}+\greenD{20}}{\purpleD{50}+\goldD{50}}=0.5 \\\\
\hat p_\text{R}&=\dfrac{\blueD{30}}{\purpleD{50}}=0.6 \\\\
\hat p_\text{L}&=\dfrac{\greenD{20}}{\goldD{50}}=0.4
\end{align}$

She wants to test $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{R}} - p_{\text{L}}>0$, so we'll use $\hat p_{\text{R}}-\hat p_{\text{L}}$ for the sample difference in the test statistic:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{(0.6-0.4)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.5(0.5)}{50}+\dfrac{0.5(0.5)}{50}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{0.2}{0.1} \\\\
&= 2.00
\end{align}$

###Calculating the P-value

Her alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}:p_{\text{R}}-p_{\text{L}}>0$. This is a one-sided test, so the P-value is the area *above* her test statistic $z=2$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. H sub a is p sub 1 minus p sub 2 greater than 0. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at z equals 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0bbd368666cc63a389e6bd41b9f5e44274933939)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z>2$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 2 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.023
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The P-value is between $0.01$ and $0.05$.

$0.01 \leq P\text{-value}<0.05$
